item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the nice comment and upvote! I'm glad you enjoyed the read. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A nice scip that made me immediately think of dryads and the male equivalent. I liked the ambiguity of the article as it allows the reader to fill in the gaps in whatever way suits their ideas about the scip. +1

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The change looks good to me! I appreciate being asked about it in chat beforehand. :)

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

After speaking with Zyn about this skip, she said she'd like it to be mostly self-contained. That said, it contains mention of a common character Zyn uses, and a crosslink can be inserted to the hub page based on said character.

Editing this:

The Foundation initially established contact with SCP-1338 when the story of an injured mountain climber recovering from several broken bones overnight prompted Foundation investigation. A small team of researchers led by Dr. Kiryu was sent to explore the region of the forest the sighting was rumored to have occurred in (see Interview 1338-1-█).

To this:

The Foundation initially established contact with SCP-1338 when the story of an injured mountain climber recovering from several broken bones overnight prompted Foundation investigation. A small team of researchers led by Dr. Kiryu was sent to explore the region of the forest the sighting was rumored to have occurred in (see Interview 1338-1-█).

Time expired, edits made as discussed above. Author may revert at any time.
I should be more careful about what I say around trees.

item.title: Re: Minor tidbit
item.content:encoded:
It is a prescriptivist rule that comes from misapplying Latin grammar to English, and I don't think is much taken seriously these days.
Or as Churchill (maybe) wrote:

“This is the sort of bloody nonsense up with which I will not put.”

Zyn:

, do you have a suggestion for how to fix the error?

If you follow the "rule", then it should be:

A perimeter is to be established around the location where SCP-1338 most frequently appears (roughly 35°45'50"N 82°15'52"W).

item.title: Re: Minor tidbit
item.content:encoded:
Is ending a sentence with a preposition grammatically incorrect? I'll fix the page if it's a serious issue, but I don't remember that being a stringent rule.

Of course, if it's more related to the text sounding properly professional, do you have a suggestion for how to fix the error?

item.title: Minor tidbit
item.content:encoded: I hate to be that guy, but you've ended your first sentence with a preposition, namely at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They lost… Harummmmmmm…. The Entwivesss

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the heads up. I've made the necessary change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The full name for the condition is Heterochromia iridum. I would have edited in myself but for some reason it is locked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, the subtlety of this was a bit difficult to work with, when I was writing this. I tend to prefer to let the reader piece things together, and in this case I felt that it would be too blunt to just throw the supposed identity of the parents at the reader.

Could you expand on the "better payoff" that could have been written?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the construction of this one and enjoyed the way it was written, but did you really have to hide the identity of the parents behind half a dozen cryptic spoilers, given the article title? This could be a really great SCP with a better payoff, but I feel like it just fell flat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like mythological characters and non-scary SCPs, but reading this one I felt like I was waiting for a twist that never came. I downvoted, reread, and then neutralized my vote— I don't dislike it, and I like the idea of a humanoid healing SCP, it just doesn't grab me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um… it's not contained on site. There's just a perimeter established around the place it usually shows up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The only thing I don't like about this is the end. It seems a little bit "lolfoundation" to me, in the sense that they should know better than to try to transplant a potentially fragile, poorly-understood sapient entity which is already well-contained on site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
However, I think that the Skip's tone needs to be reworked, particularly in the second interview log. A centuries-old druid child who lives in the woods probably wouldn't speak like a typical 21st century American.

Fair point, though I numbered the interview to indicate which meeting it would have taken place during (9), which would indicate nine months of having been in contact with the Foundation. Granted, I intended to have a sort of contrast between the first and second logs, so I might change the tone of the skip slightly in the first interview.

Wow I seem to be adding a lot of these in this thread
hide

Also, the "child" is a child in pretty much appearance only. His "age" is relative—for his particular species of tree, he's rather young, and so his human-looking form implies that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have any strong feelings about this article as a whole one way or the other (it's sort of bland, but inoffensive as far as humanoids articles go). However, I think that the Skip's tone needs to be reworked, particularly in the second interview log. A centuries-old druid child who lives in the woods probably wouldn't speak like a typical 21st century American.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you're totally allowed to use a specific location if you really want to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dates, names and exact location are usually blacked out even from the containment procedures

Granted, I've never seen an exact location given in containment procedures before (I find they're usually in the description?) but you have a point. If anything, it makes me feel better about people pointing out "you can't use that location! It's too close to a certain conspicuously tall mountain!".

As such, I'm not terribly familiar with coordinates anyway, so the blackboxes do seem valid, in retrospect. I'll change it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dates, names and exact location are usually blacked out even from the containment procedures (this is the only acceptable redaction there). Leaving in only the degrees and the N/W and blacking out the minutes and seconds tells the reader that it's in NC, yet leaves a roughly 8000 km2 area.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If it wasn't in the containment procedures, I would have done so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would blackbox the coordinates (3█°██'██"N, 8█°██'██"W, or so). It's too easy for some NC-based members to visit the actual location.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoo boy. This ended up longer than I thought it would.

replies
hide

the specification in parenthesis should be detailing the depth, not the length

The lack of specification regarding the depth was to imply that the cuts were not intentionally inflicted as part of a test, but rather just a random result of researchers trekking around a mountain for several days. The reason I gave lengths was to give the reader a rough idea of how much surface area of skin would need to be repaired. I'm not too sure if measuring the depth of something akin to a paper cut would be viable, as it is.

After all, this only applies to "shallow" cuts

Not necessarily. The Foundation didn't have any research members with severe injuries on hand when they first encountered SCP-1338. Remember the man with broken bones who was healed overnight? There aren't any tests being done regarding the healing (which isn't so much "healing" as it is just using a mix of natural drugs to speed up natural processes) because the Foundation has figured out that SCP-1338 doesn't treat people out of generosity, but to get them out of the forest.

so what determines them to be shallow?

As far as I know there's no specific scientific measurement of a constant that would determine whether a cut can be considered shallow or not; my personal take was that there would be superficial bleeding and it'd sting a little. I assumed that most people would have a definition of "shallow cut" either similar or less severe than that.

The Foundation likes things to be precise.

I'm trying not to call you out, but in this case, I personally didn't feel the detail to be immediately significant. Also, it doesn't seem natural that someone would bother to measure the depth of a small cut right before getting it treated. If you were thinking something more along the lines of the skin layers that were damaged (i.e. epidermis), I believe "shallow" would be rather self-explanatory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shallow cuts (ranging from 1 to 4 cm long) treated by SCP-1338 heal completely after an average of four minutes

If you're gonna talk about the shallow cuts (the specification in parenthesis should be detailing the depth, not the length) After all, this only applies to "shallow" cuts, so what determines them to be shallow? The Foundation likes things to be precise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is nicely done. Congratulations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
is also a spoiler cut
hide

Yes. Every member of the family is a tree. The friends are also trees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the mother

is a spoiler cut
– hide block

is a tree. Is the mother a tree?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one.

It's like the Foundation found a folklore/myth character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh. It feels like I've seen all of the obvious elements before, and the less-obvious ones don't engage me. -1, was not entertained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hahaha, yeah, there's a lot of ways this could be interpreted. It was hard for me to decide what to keep and what to trim out.

Hmm...
– hide block

Yes, I intended the father to be an evergreen, and a strong one at that, so he was cut down to be used to build something. (Something rather important, actually. A ship carrying people to a new world, and if you know the historical context for that, you know about how long the child has been trying to learn the healing abilities required to restore his father from whatever's left of him.

And yes, well done! The reason the Foundation can't locate him otherwise is because he occupies two forms, something that his father's "friends" taught him to do. He can move freely now, but once he grows older, he won't be able to leave his tree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahaha, I got what you were hinting at, mostly. I thought

+ show block
– hide block

That the father was transplanted alive for some reason, and was an evergreen as opposed to a sort of tree with a seasonal cycle. Just a question, though - is SCP-1338 able to move around more or less freely because he's still young, and when he hits adulthood he'll be rooted for good?

Anyway, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really not drawn in by this, unfortunately. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you strike a good balance between allusive and explicit with regards to the SCP's origins. I also liked the general feel of the article—very evocative, despite the compulsory clinical tone. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually didn't intentionally base this off of any particular lore.

Well...
hide

Except maybe the concept of dryads. SCP-1338 is male, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm unfamiliar with lore and such, so could you spoiler me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Incidentally, a few people I showed this to initially thought that the parents were both deities.

Also, in regards to the Blue Ridge mountains…

MOAR!Spoilers
hide

The reason I picked that location was because of its relative proximity to the east coast of the United States (as opposed to, say the Rocky Mountains). The other location SCP-1338 was seen in, a forest in England is also significant, in that the family originally lived together in one of those locations before humans interfered. Putting together the backstory reveals for how long now the child has tried to take care of his father. Also, in regards to that, SCP-1338 appears as a child because his aging process is so slow, due to his being, well, not human.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it once I understood it. Initially I thought the father and mother might be Hades and Persephone, but I soon realized that was silly and didn't fit at all. I also like how the SCP usually shows up in the Blue Ridge mountains, but that's mostly because they're the setting of many a fond memory of mine and has little to do with the merit of the writing itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it. Nice concept. I also like Dr. Kiryu. And all the little tidbits scattered about in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Generally, I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks to everyone in chat who reviewed this and gave me critique, especially Scantron, Kens, Nusquam (Adam?), Vivax, Jekeled, and Bright. (and Moose.)

If you don’t understand the SCP, perhaps look at the small hints for context?

Spoilers?
Spoilerrssss

Do you have an idea of who the parents are? If not, consider this: drawing from details in the addenda (the reasons for the father's love, and why the mother stayed while the child left), what changes with the seasons and is rooted to the spot?

The answer is what the family members are, and explains why the father was taken away from the mother and child. Also, what can both boats and wagons be made of? That’s what happened to the father.

The child himself is attempting to learn to heal others so he can bring back his father, from whatever’s left of him. He heals anything nearby when he can, not out of generosity, but because he needs to practice. The reason for those rectangular shaped cells? Plant cells. His healing isn’t exactly suited for animals, and if anything, he doesn’t particularly care for them.

Also, the reason he can’t be contained in a place outside his forest is the same reason my potted bamboo died right after I brought it home for winter break.
